title: Python SDK: streaming chat breaks when tool calls appear (JSON decode error)
preview: Customer uses tool calling with streaming chat. When the model emits a tool_call, the Python SDK streaming iterator raises JSONDecodeError while parsing an SSE chunk. Customer provided the raw chunk which includes an escaped newline inside the tool arguments.
text: Customer uses tool calling with streaming chat. When the model emits a tool_call, the Python SDK streaming iterator raises JSONDecodeError while parsing an SSE chunk. Customer provided the raw chunk which includes an escaped newline inside the tool arguments.

title: SEV-2: eu-west latency spike after runtime build 2026.03.18.0 (kernel selection)
preview:
Issue summary: p95 latency regression for long-context traffic in eu-west after runtime build 2026.03.18.0.

Impact: SLO burn for chat long-context bucket; customers saw slower responses but low error rate.

Environment: prod eu-west, A100 pools.

Investigation notes: Suspected kernel selection heuristic change; perf-canary shows regression aligned with build.

Mitigation: Disable heuristic via feature flag; force kernel to prior stable variant.
text:
Issue summary: p95 latency regression for long-context traffic in eu-west after runtime build 2026.03.18.0.

Impact: SLO burn for chat long-context bucket; customers saw slower responses but low error rate.

Environment: prod eu-west, A100 pools.

Investigation notes: Suspected kernel selection heuristic change; perf-canary shows regression aligned with build.

Mitigation: Disable heuristic via feature flag; force kernel to prior stable variant.

title: PulseRx — mid-stream token interleaving from multiplexer misrouting causing garbled streaming completions and spike in billable tokens
preview: Issue summary: During PulseRx's morning ingest window (09:30-10:15 UTC, 2026-03-11) a subset of streaming completions returned interleaved token sequences and occasionally duplicated segments from concurrent requests. This produced garbled patient-facing summaries and inflated token counts on billing. Impact: high — customer-facing clinical summarization pipeline produced invalid notes for ~0.9% of requests in the window, and PulseRx reports a ~22% increase in billable tokens for the same period.
text: Issue summary: During PulseRx's morning ingest window (09:30-10:15 UTC, 2026-03-11) a subset of streaming completions returned interleaved token sequences and occasionally duplicated segments from concurrent requests. This produced garbled patient-facing summaries and inflated token counts on billing. Impact: high — customer-facing clinical summarization pipeline produced invalid notes for ~0.9% of requests in the window, and PulseRx reports a ~22% increase in billable tokens for the same period.

title: Intermittent 429 bursts for multiplexed websocket chat causing degraded UX
preview: Issue summary: NimbusHealth is seeing intermittent bursts of HTTP 429 responses returned to multiplexed websocket clients on the hosted API during peak business hours. Impact: Users in their clinical triage chat are experiencing message failures and UI retries, increasing perceived latency and leading to dropped conversations. Scope: Affects ~10 client connection pools from NimbusHealth (enterprise account) in us-east region; seen across redwood-chat-small and medium models. Expected: Multiplexed websocket connections should see smoothing by token-level and connection-level rate limits with transparent backpressure, not sudden 429 bursts. Observed: Spikes of 429s lasting 20-40s every ~15 minutes, correlating with bursty fan-out from a single internal service. Steps to reproduce: 1) Establish multiplexed websocket session using shared API key for multiple browser clients (as customer implemented). 2) Simulate parallel message bursts (20 simultaneous requests over the same websocket multiplex) with messages that produce ~80-200 tokens. 3) Observe intermittent 429 responses returned with X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 and Retry-After headers. Logs: Attached sample gateway headers and rate-limiter trace (see comment #2). Customer note: This started after migration to a client-side multiplexer used to reduce websocket count. Customer requests guidance on configuration to avoid disruption without dramatically lowering throughput.
text: Issue summary: NimbusHealth is seeing intermittent bursts of HTTP 429 responses returned to multiplexed websocket clients on the hosted API during peak business hours. Impact: Users in their clinical triage chat are experiencing message failures and UI retries, increasing perceived latency and leading to dropped conversations. Expected: Multiplexed websocket connections should see smoothing by token-level and connection-level rate limits with transparent backpressure, not sudden 429 bursts. Steps to reproduce: 1) Establish multiplexed websocket session using shared API key for multiple browser clients (as customer implemented). 2) Simulate parallel message bursts (20 simultaneous requests over the same websocket multiplex) with messages that produce ~80-200 tokens. 3) Observe intermittent 429 responses returned with X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 and Retry-After headers. Logs: Attached sample gateway headers and rate-limiter trace (see comment #2). Customer note: This started after migration to a client-side multiplexer used to reduce websocket count. Customer requests guidance on configuration to avoid disruption without dramatically lowering throughput.

title: Buffer credit escrowed after org consolidation refunded to wrong billing profile, provisional negative balance
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports that after a planned org consolidation on 2026-03-10, a held hourly buffer credit (warmup/escrow) was released but applied to an unrelated billing profile within the consolidated tenant. This produced a provisional negative balance on the customer's primary billing profile and blocked autopay on the next cycle.

Impact:
- StellarVoice reported a provisional negative balance of $-1,234.56 on their primary billing profile; autopay blocked.
- Their production inference traffic in us-east experienced quota throttling for ~3 hours while finance blocked charges.
- Customer requests a full reconciliation audit and corrective credit move rather than a blanket refund.

Environment:
- Redwood prod (us-east region)
- Consolidation event executed via org-service migration job on 2026-03-10 02:10 UTC

Expected behavior:
- Held buffer/warmup credits associated with an API key or subaccount should migrate with the corresponding billing profile or be flagged for manual reassignment when consolidation maps keys to a different profile. Credits should not be auto-applied to unrelated billing profiles.

Actual behavior:
- Escrowed buffer credits were released and attributed to a different billing profile created during consolidation (billing_profile_789). The original billing profile (billing_profile_123) shows missing credits and provisional negative balance.

Steps to reproduce / observed timeline:
1) 2026-03-10 01:50 UTC - Org consolidation job started (migration job ID: org-mig-20260310-01).
2) 2026-03-10 02:10 UTC - Postback reconciliation worker processed buffered escrow records for tenant.
3) 2026-03-10 02:15 UTC - Credits released; credit-escrow records assigned to billing_profile_789.
4) 2026-03-10 03:00 UTC - Customer noticed autopay block; opened support chat.
5) 2026-03-10 04:10 UTC - Customer escalated to account team.

Relevant logs and evidence:
- Postback reconciler logs (postback-worker-11): "escrow_record id=esc-44321 assigned_profile=billing_profile_789 source=org-mig-20260310-01"
- Billing audit: "credit_adjustment: -1234.56 from billing_profile_123 (orphaned)"
- Console screenshot attached by customer showing negative balance and blocked autopay timestamped 2026-03-10 03:05 UTC.

Customer request:
- Move escrowed credits back to billing_profile_123 or apply equivalent credit to that profile.
- Provide reconciliation CSV with mapping and timestamps, and explain root cause so customer can update internal finance records.

title: Go SDK: pointer-omitempty JSON divergence causes idempotency key mismatch for function calls
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers upgrading of a Go microservice to the latest go-sdk are seeing duplicate external tool executions after retries. The duplicates occur only when the initial request is routed from a Go client and the retry originates from a Python/TS orchestrator (or vice-versa).

Impact:
- Inkline AI reported 3 duplicate downstream webhook executions over a 24h window resulting in duplicate invoices being created in their billing system.
- Occurs intermittently on high-concurrency request paths where retries/backoff trigger cross-language replay.

Environment:
- Affected customer: Inkline AI (dedicated)
- Region: us-east (prod)
- SDKs observed: go-sdk v0.9.3 -> v0.9.5 (regression), python-sdk 1.12.0, ts-sdk 2.5.1
- Endpoint: /v1/chat/function_call with JSON-encoded arguments that include optional fields (e.g., metadata, callback_url)

What we believe is happening (short):
The platform computes per-request idempotency keys by hashing a canonical serialization of the function-call payload (model, input, function name, arguments). The Go SDK sometimes omits zero-value fields when encoding structs (common pattern using `omitempty` on pointer/optional fields), while Python/TS SDKs include explicit null/empty values. The differing JSON results in different hashes so the server treats a retry from the other-language client as a new request, causing duplicate tool executions.
text:
Issue summary:
Customers upgrading of a Go microservice to the latest go-sdk are seeing duplicate external tool executions after retries. The duplicates occur only when the initial request is routed from a Go client and the retry originates from a Python/TS orchestrator (or vice-versa).

Impact:
- Inkline AI reported 3 duplicate downstream webhook executions over a 24h window resulting in duplicate invoices being created in their billing system.
- Occurs intermittently on high-concurrency request paths where retries/backoff trigger cross-language replay.

Environment:
- Affected customer: Inkline AI (dedicated)
- Region: us-east (prod)
- SDKs observed: go-sdk v0.9.3 -> v0.9.5 (regression), python-sdk 1.12.0, ts-sdk 2.5.1
- Endpoint: /v1/chat/function_call with JSON-encoded arguments that include optional fields (e.g., metadata, callback_url)

What we believe is happening (short):
The platform computes per-request idempotency keys by hashing a canonical serialization of the function-call payload (model, input, function name, arguments). The Go SDK sometimes omits zero-value fields when encoding structs (common pattern using `omitempty` on pointer/optional fields), while Python/TS SDKs include explicit null/empty values. The differing JSON results in different hashes so the server treats a retry from the other-language client as a new request, causing duplicate tool executions.

title: Dedicated cluster: GPU contention causing sustained throughput collapse and autoscale oscillation
preview:
Issue summary:
Scanly Labs reports a sharp, sustained drop in throughput from their dedicated GPU pool in us-west starting 2026-03-11 09:12 UTC. Latency for streaming generation requests increases and many requests time out. The Redwood autoscaler began oscillating (rapid scale-up then scale-down) around the same time, making the throughput degradation persistent.

Impact:
- Production traffic for Scanly Labs' inference endpoints degraded: 60-80% drop in token throughput on pinned routes.
- Errors observed: tail latency spikes >10s, client-side timeouts, and a small increase in 5xx from serving-runtime.
- Customer-facing feature (real-time report generation) impacted for ~80% of requests routed to dedicated pool.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: scanly-prod-pool-usw (reserved 12 GPUs across 3 nodes)
- Region: us-west
- Models: gptj-compat-13b (primary), redwood-rlhf-7b (fallback)

Observed behaviour / logs summary:
- Prometheus graphs show GPU utilization uneven: some GPUs pegged near 98% memory usage, others at 5-10% idle.
- Node exporter shows frequent GPU ECC/corrected error increases on node gpu-02-usw.
- Autoscaler logs: frequent scale events every 3-7 minutes triggered by queue growth, followed by immediate scale-down when some requests fail with GPU allocation error.
- Kubelet logs on affected nodes contain repeated container OOM and GPU allocation timeout messages.

Steps to reproduce (customer pattern):
1) Send steady production traffic to pinned model route for 30+ minutes (Scanly load test reproduced issue).
2) Observe queue length and latency grow, autoscaler adds capacity.
3) Some newly added nodes immediately show GPU allocation failures; scheduler reschedules pods causing node churn and eviction spikes.

Initial hypothesis:
- A single tenant process on one node is causing GPU memory fragmentation or a driver-level fault that prevents the scheduler from placing pods evenly, leading to practical GPU starvation on some nodes (noisy-tenant-like behaviour).
- Autoscaler is reacting to queue length but not accounting for allocation failures, so it oscillates and amplifies instability.
- Potential hardware/driver issue on gpu-02-usw (ECC counters, thermal events) contributing to allocation timeouts.

Customer's immediate ask:
- Restore stable throughput and stop autoscale oscillation.
- Short-term mitigation while root cause is identified.

References: slack #support-scanly (thread TS12345), infra PR 2345, Confluence runbook above.

title: Unexpected 'SLA latency penalty' surcharge on finalized invoice after hosted fallback during region failover
preview:
Issue summary:
Acme Health (dedicated) found a charge labeled 'SLA latency penalty — us-east-1 — 2026-03-08 02:15–05:30' for $432.15 on the finalized invoice for billing cycle ending 2026-03-08. The customer experienced a region failover and automatic hosted fallback for ~3 hours. They assert that SLA latency penalties should not apply to dedicated customers while fallback is active, and that any surrogate routing should inherit exemption tags from the reserved contract.

Impact:
- Customer-facing: unexpected invoice line and total increase (~$432.15).
- Financial: customer requests immediate provisional credit while we investigate.
- Trust: customer concerned about billing rule correctness during fallback events.

Environment:
- Customer tier: Dedicated
- Primary region: us-east-1
- Event window: 2026-03-08 02:15–05:30 UTC
- Service components involved: smart-routing -> hosted fallback -> postback-reconciler -> billing pipeline

Steps to reproduce / observed timeline:
1) 2026-03-08 02:14 UTC: automated region failover triggered for us-east-1 due to maintenance window (see INC-20260308-01).
2) 02:15 UTC: Smart router applied fallback policy and routed traffic to hosted pool. Route tags at time of routing show fallback tag 'hosted:fallback-us-east'.
3) 02:18–05:10 UTC: customer traffic served via hosted pool; reserved capacity was saturated.
4) 05:30 UTC: traffic returned to dedicated pool and routing tag reverted.
5) 2026-03-09: Billing pipeline evaluated SLA windows and emitted a 'sla_penalty' invoice line attributed to Acme Health for the 02:15–05:30 window.
6) 2026-03-10: Customer received finalized invoice containing surcharge.

Logs / evidence attached (snippets below):
- smart-router: "[2026-03-08T02:15:11Z] route=acme-health fallback=hosted:fallback-us-east reason=region-failover"
- postback-reconciler: "[2026-03-08T02:16:30Z] tag=hosted:fallback-us-east org_id=acme-123 mapped_rate=hosted-default"
- billing: "[2026-03-09T03:02:12Z] rule_eval=sla_penalty window=2026-03-08T02:15:00Z..2026-03-08T05:30:00Z target_org=acme-123 amount=432.15"

Additional context:
Customer expectation: fallback routing should not create SLA-penalty charges for dedicated customers; fallback is intended to preserve availability and inherit reserved billing/exemptions. Customer provided console screenshots showing route history and a billing line item.

Requested outcome:
1) Immediate provisional credit for the surcharge while we validate.
2) Root cause analysis and permanent fix if billing misattributed the penalty.
3) Clear documentation of how SLA penalty rules apply across reserved/dedicated-to-hosted fallbacks.

title: Workstation experiencing thermal shutdowns and 0 RPM fan telemetry after BIOS firmware rollout
preview:
Issue summary:
Several corporate developer laptops started reporting fan RPM as 0 in telemetry and have experienced thermal throttling or unexpected shutdowns after the March 8th BIOS firmware push. Affected devices are primarily Lenovo ThinkPad fleet that received the v1.2.3 BIOS rollback/upgrade during nightly autopilot/intune maintenance window.

Impact:
- Multiple engineers reported sudden shutdowns while compiling or running local model-serving containers.
- Telemetry shows CPU package temps > 95C and fan RPM 0 prior to shutdown.
- A subset of devices are recoverable by power-cycling; others require manual reseating of fan cable or BIOS re-flash.

Environment:
- corp-laptop | us-west region endpoints registered in Intune
- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 22H2 with latest cumulative updates
- Endpoint management: Intune + Autopilot + custom vendor BIOS automation job

title: CI Terraform run blocked: CMEK/KMS keyRing and key creation denied by org-policy during dedicated-prod onboarding
preview:
Issue summary:
CI (terraform) provisioning step that creates a KMS KeyRing and CMEK key in project redwood-prod-dedicated failed with org-policy DENY on keyRing.create and serviceAccount.impersonate. This blocks onboarding of analytics-replicator-sa for Dedicated environment and prevents terraform apply from completing.

Impact:
- New Dedicated capacity onboarding is blocked (analytics-replicator service) delaying customer migration.
- CI pipeline failing on every run; engineers cannot complete infra changes requiring CMEK.
- Risk of manual key creation and out-of-band grants if not resolved quickly.

Environment:
- GCP project: redwood-prod-dedicated
- CI: github-actions self-hosted runners in prod-us-west
- Terraform version: 1.6.8; google provider: 4.79.0
- Affected regions: us-west1 (primary), us-east1 (replica)

Steps to reproduce:
1. Checkout branch infra/cmek/onboard-analytics
2. Trigger CI workflow .github/workflows/provision-cmek.yaml or run `terraform apply` with service account impersonation from the runner.
3. Observe error during `google_kms_key_ring` creation step.

Observed logs / errors:
- iam.serviceAccounts.signBlob denied for impersonation attempts (logs attached in CI run).
- Error: "googleapi: Error 403: Permission 'resourcemanager.projects.create' denied by organization policy 'constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation'" (note: message varies depending on provider call).
- Audit log excerpt: orgpolicy.v1 DenyViolation on projects/redwood-prod-dedicated: constraint=constraints/gcp.restrictResourceCreation, denied_action=cloudkms.keyRings.create

Investigation notes:
- Org policy audit shows a recently-applied policy (GCP Org Policy: restrictResourceCreation -> permittedResources limited to team-managed projects) that blocks programmatic creation of KMS KeyRings unless the request originates from a whitelisted principal.
- CI runner uses impersonation of ci-service-sa to perform terraform; the org-policy expects either an approved infra-service account or a signed approval tag on the request.
- Cross-referenced with INT-572913 which flagged a similar deny pattern for a different project; that ticket included an org-policy change by Security on 2026-03-01.
- Hypothesis: Security applied a tightened policy during the compliance sweep; Dedicated onboarding path did not get the required exception.

Temporary mitigation / workaround:
- Manual creation: Infra can manually create the KeyRing and CMEK via an owner account and then grant the required roles to analytics-replicator-sa. This is sensitive and requires audit entry and rotation plan.
- Alternative: run Terraform with a privileged admin service account (not recommended long-term) and then reduce privileges after provisioning.

Proposed remediation steps:
1. Confirm with Security whether the org-policy change on 2026-03-01 is intentional for all projects or scoped to org-folder.
2. If intentional, request a scoped exception for projects/redwood-prod-dedicated for the resource types cloudkms.keyRings.create and cloudkms.cryptoKeys.create for the infra provisioning principal (ci-provisioner-sa) for 48 hours to allow onboarding.
3. Update infra onboarding docs to include the required pre-created resources and the approved SA impersonation list.
4. Post-fix: run terraform apply from CI and validate audit logs show the provisioning principal and not a human owner.
5. If exception is not approved, plan documented manual provisioning runbook and AM/PM rotation for keys.

Next steps / owners:
- Reach out to Security (tag: @security-org-policies) to validate policy intent and request exception.
- Infra/Platform (assignee Priya Natarajan) to prepare justification and minimum-scope exception request and to test manual create steps if required.
- Compliance to log the manual intervention if chosen.

SLA notes:
- Customer impact: internal onboarding delay only, but flagged P1 due to pipeline-blocking nature.
- First response due by: 2026-03-10 (same day triage).
text:

CI (terraform) provisioning step that creates a KMS KeyRing and CMEK key in project redwood-prod-dedicated failed with org-policy DENY on keyRing.create and serviceAccount.impersonate. Investigation notes:
- Org policy audit shows a recently-applied policy (GCP Org Policy: restrictResourceCreation -> permittedResources limited to team-managed projects) that blocks programmatic creation of KMS KeyRings unless the request originates from a whitelisted principal.
- CI runner uses impersonation of ci-service-sa to perform terraform; the org-policy expects either an approved infra-service account or a signed approval tag on the request.
- Cross-referenced with INT-572913 which flagged a similar deny pattern for a different project; that ticket included an org-policy change by Security on 2026-03-01.
- Hypothesis: Security applied a tightened policy during the compliance sweep; Dedicated onboarding path did not get the required exception.

Temporary mitigation / workaround:
- Manual creation: Infra can manually create the KeyRing and CMEK via an owner account and then grant the required roles to analytics-replicator-sa. This is sensitive and requires audit entry and rotation plan.
- Alternative: run Terraform with a privileged admin service account (not recommended long-term) and then reduce privileges after provisioning.

Proposed remediation steps:
1. Confirm with Security whether the org-policy change on 2026-03-01 is intentional for all projects or scoped to org-folder.
2. If intentional, request a scoped exception for projects/redwood-prod-dedicated for the resource types cloudkms.keyRings.create and cloudkms.cryptoKeys.create for the infra provisioning principal (ci-provisioner-sa) for 48 hours to allow onboarding.
3. Update infra onboarding docs to include the required pre-created resources and the approved SA impersonation list.
4. Post-fix: run terraform apply from CI and validate audit logs show the provisioning principal and not a human owner.
5. If exception is not approved, plan documented manual provisioning runbook and AM/PM rotation for keys.

Next steps / owners:
- Reach out to Security (tag: @security-org-policies) to validate policy intent and request exception.
- Infra/Platform (assignee Priya Natarajan) to prepare justification and minimum-scope exception request and to test manual create steps if required.
- Compliance to log the manual intervention if chosen.

SLA notes:
- Customer impact: internal onboarding delay only, but flagged P1 due to pipeline-blocking nature.
- First response due by: 2026-03-10 (same day triage).

title: VPCE conditional-forwarder mismatch causing stale service IPs -> healthcheck flapping and slow canary rollbacks
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer (Finlytics Corp) on a Redwood Private deployment reports intermittent serving node healthcheck flaps and delayed canary rollbacks after a routine control-plane endpoint IP change. Investigation shows control-plane A records propagated to the customer's VPC endpoint inconsistently due to a conditional-forwarder mismatch on their branch DNS resolver chain.

Impact:
- Several short-lived model serving canary rollouts experienced prolonged degraded states (canary stayed in 'degrading' for 6-12 minutes instead of expected ~30s).
- Occasional 502/504 on model inference paths during artifact fetch retries when a subset of serving nodes resolved control-plane to old IPs and attempted auth refreshes against retired endpoints.
- Customer reports user-facing latency spikes on scheduled batch jobs for ~2 hours during the propagation window.

Environment:
- Private deployment via VPCE (Finlytics VPC)
- Region: us-east-1
- Redwood control-plane domain: control.redwood-inference.internal (internal CNAMEs to control-plane-azX.us-east-1.rw)
- Customer DNS chain: branch-forwarder -> onprem-resolver -> VPCE-resolver (conditional forwarder configured for *.redwood-inference.internal)

Steps to reproduce:
1) Customer triggers a control-plane small IP rotation (internal maintenance) at 02:10 UTC.
2) On a subset of branch resolvers that do NOT include the new delegated zone mapping, lookups for control.redwood-inference.internal continue to return cached A records for retired IPs (TTL remaining) while VPCE-resolver returns new IPs.
3) Compare healthcheck target resolution on serving nodes: nodes using the stale path intermittently fail control-plane healthchecks (TLS connection attempts succeed but mTLS handshake errors due to server cert CN mismatch when connecting to decommissioned hostnames).

Observed behavior:
- Split-view DNS: some hosts resolve control-plane to new IPs, others to old IPs; result is mixed client behavior.
- Serving node health checks flip between OK/Degraded as connections route to mismatched endpoints.
- Canary controller waits for consistent healthy signals and eventually rolls back slowly due to cloud-provider-level retry/backoff.

Expected behavior:
- Conditional forwarder and resolver chain should produce a consistent view (new IPs) within TTL window and we should not see certificate name mismatches during a small control-plane rotation.

Logs / snippets (redacted):
- Serving node TLS client: "Connect to 10.23.41.12:443 - TLS handshake completed - server cert CN=control-az1-20260210.internal (expected control-az2-*)"
- Control-plane auth service: "Auth refresh attempt from node i-0a1b2c3d failed: 403 token validation: client presented old cert (CN mismatch)"
- Customer resolver logs: "Query: control.redwood-inference.internal -> forwarded to onprem-resolver (via conditional forwarder)"

Temporary workaround applied by Support:
- Asked customer to purge conditional-forwarder cache and lower DNS cache TTL temporarily on branch resolvers (from 3600s -> 60s) to accelerate convergence.
- Applied a short-lived control-plane compatibility window by re-adding retired IPs to TLS SANs for 10 minutes during the fix window (coord with infra ops).

Notes on sensitivity:
- This manifests only during control-plane IP rotations and on resolver chains using conditional forwarders with stale delegation records. Not a Redwood runtime bug but a private-deploy operational edge case requiring DNS coordination with customer network teams.

title: Request: Append-only signed audit stream with sequence proofs plus SIEM field mapping and monthly retention attestations
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer requests a machine-verifiable, append-only audit export for legal review and SIEM ingestion. They need sequence proofs and detached signatures per archive so auditors can verify immutability and ordering.

Impact:
Aurelia Health requires cryptographic proof of log immutability plus a field-level mapping for their SIEM. Delay blocks their SOC attestation and quarterly compliance milestone.

Environment:
- Redwood hosted API (prod, us-east)
- Customer tenant: aurelia-prod (enterprise)

Customer request (detailed):
1) A daily export (UTC) of all tenant-scoped audit events for March 2026 in JSONL, delivered to the customer's SFTP endpoint.
2) Each daily archive must be accompanied by:
   - A detached signature (PKCS7 or OpenPGP) OR an HMAC-SHA256 manifest signed with a per-customer KMS key.
   - A sequence manifest listing the start and end sequence numbers for that day and a SHA256 of the gzipped archive.
   - A chaining token / hash so auditors can verify the chain of daily archives (e.g., prev_hash included in each manifest).
3) A field mapping document that maps Redwood audit event keys to SIEM fields expected by the customer's Splunk ingestion pipeline. Include examples and sample syslog lines.
4) A short attestation document signed by Redwood security: encryption at rest details, key IDs used, retention policy applied to the tenant, and deletion-proof procedures.
5) If signed manifests are not yet supported, provide a timeframe for implementation and an interim attest/offset-based proof that allows auditor verification (e.g., per-request sequence IDs with stable offsets).

Steps to reproduce / validate (for Support/Eng):
- Confirm storage bucket(s) for tenant aurelia-prod and list objects for the requested date range (2026-03-01 to 2026-03-31).
- Produce sample gzipped JSONL for a single day and compute SHA256.
- Produce a manifest file with fields: date, tenant_id, start_sequence, end_sequence, archive_sha256, prev_manifest_sha256, signed_by_kms_key_id.
- Provide sample verification command lines for auditor (gpg --verify or openssl dgst -sha256 -verify).

Requested delivery:
- Provide the March export or a clear delivery plan and ETA. Customer prefers SFTP + detached signature. If SFTP is not possible, a secure presigned URL per archive is acceptable but must still include detached signature and manifest.

Additional notes on SIEM mapping expected by customer (examples):
- timestamp -> @timestamp (ISO8601)
- tenant_id -> tenant_id
- request_id -> req_id
- actor.principal -> user_email
- action -> event.action
- model -> model.name
- model_version -> model.version
- latency_ms -> latency_ms
- response_tokens -> response.tokens
- bytes_out -> network.bytes_out
- src_ip -> client.ip
- auth_result -> auth.outcome
- outcome -> event.outcome
- redwood_signature -> signature_detached

Wanted artifacts:
- One day sample archive + detached signature + manifest (for proof of concept).
- Field mapping document in CSV or JSON schema form.
- Security attestation PDF.

title: Intermittent model artifact fetch failures from private deploy due to split-horizon DNS + TGW route propagation race causing OCSP lookup mismatch
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent failures fetching model artifacts from S3/HTTP origin during private deployment. Failures correlate with NAT/TGW route churn and control-plane DNS conditional forwarding. Impact: model pulls fail sporadically causing cold-start timeouts and 5xx for in-flight requests. Environment: private deployment in Acme AI VPC connected to Redwood control plane via Transit Gateway and a pair of NAT gateways in the egress subnet.

Observed behaviour: when upstream route propagation completes, DNS results from split-horizon forwarder flip between an internal resolver (that rewrites OCSP responders to private IPs) and public resolution. At the same time ephemeral egress IPs are reassigned during NAT replacement, producing transient TLS stapling verification failures and OCSP timeouts, which our artifact fetch code treats as fatal rather than retryable.
text:
Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent failures fetching model artifacts from S3/HTTP origin during private deployment. Failures correlate with NAT/TGW route churn and control-plane DNS conditional forwarding. Impact: model pulls fail sporadically causing cold-start timeouts and 5xx for in-flight requests. Environment: private deployment in Acme AI VPC connected to Redwood control plane via Transit Gateway and a pair of NAT gateways in the egress subnet.

Observed behaviour: when upstream route propagation completes, DNS results from split-horizon forwarder flip between an internal resolver (that rewrites OCSP responders to private IPs) and public resolution. At the same time ephemeral egress IPs are reassigned during NAT replacement, producing transient TLS stapling verification failures and OCSP timeouts, which our artifact fetch code treats as fatal rather than retryable.

title: Connection pool exhaustion causing sustained 504 waves from API gateway
preview:
Issue summary: Starting ~2026-03-10 03:15 UTC we observed sustained waves of 504 Gateway Timeouts returned to customers in the dedicated environment. Impact: ~8% of requests to the customer's pinned model returned 504 over a 90-minute window, causing request retries and user-visible failures. Environment: Dedicated tenant (NimbusAnalytics) running in prod us-east-1, pinned to gpt-open-13b-v2. Initial symptom: API gateway returned 504 with upstream connection errors.

High level hypothesis: API gateway reported upstream connection pool exhaustion — idle connections were not being returned to the pool promptly because long-lived streaming sessions (some aborted by clients) did not fully release sockets on the serving-runtime side. This caused connection queues at the gateway to fill and new requests to be dropped with 504s until connections closed.
text:
Issue summary: Starting ~2026-03-10 03:15 UTC we observed sustained waves of 504 Gateway Timeouts returned to customers in the dedicated environment. Impact: ~8% of requests to the customer's pinned model returned 504 over a 90-minute window, causing request retries and user-visible failures. Environment: Dedicated tenant (NimbusAnalytics) running in prod us-east-1, pinned to gpt-open-13b-v2. Initial symptom: API gateway returned 504 with upstream connection errors.

High level hypothesis: API gateway reported upstream connection pool exhaustion — idle connections were not being returned to the pool promptly because long-lived streaming sessions (some aborted by clients) did not fully release sockets on the serving-runtime side. This caused connection queues at the gateway to fill and new requests to be dropped with 504s until connections closed.

title: RRB-17: Private installer rollback loop / stuck rollback during upgrade
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers upgrading Redwood Private 2.8.0 -> 2.9.0 can enter an infinite rollback retry loop. UI shows "Rollback in progress" and never completes.

Impact:
- Upgrade/rollback does not converge; customer cluster remains in degraded state.
- Support load + customer trust risk.

Environment:
- Private installer 2.9.0
- Kubernetes (various), observed in us-east + eu-west

Suspected root cause:
Rollback cleanup hook exits before clearing rollback lock (ConfigMap `installer-rollback-lock`), causing controller to retry indefinitely.

Workaround (customer-safe):
1) Confirm namespace `redwood-private`
2) `kubectl -n redwood-private delete cm installer-rollback-lock`
3) `kubectl -n redwood-private rollout restart deploy/installer-controller`

Acceptance criteria / fix:
- Always best-effort clear lock
- Add TTL + loop detection to stop retries and surface actionable status
- Add metric + alert when rollback retries >3 in 10m

Comments:
- 2026-02-12 Jordan Blake: Related to incident INC-2147. See Slack #incidents thread_ts=1770937323.
- 2026-02-13 Priya Natarajan: Patch in progress; will land in 2.9.1. PR: redwood-private-installer#842.
- 2026-02-14 Dev Patel: Added metric `installer.rollback.loop_detected` and proposed alert threshold.

title: Office printer island - intermittent print queue failures and network timeouts
preview:
Issue summary: Several staff on floor 3 (printer island A/B/C) report intermittent failures when submitting print jobs. Jobs appear in the client queue but never reach the printer; sometimes jobs error with a network timeout or 'printer not found'.

Impact: Low-medium operational impact; employees are using floor printers for contracts and physical sign-offs. Reported across 6 users over the last 48 hours; not a full outage but recurring and disruptive.

Environment: On-prem office network, wired VLAN 12 (office-devices), printers are HP LaserJet managed via local print server (windows-printsvc-03). Office DHCP and printer IPs appear stable. Printers are assigned static reservations.

Steps to reproduce:
1. From a macOS or Windows client on VLAN 12, submit a 20-page print job to Printer-Island-A.
2. Observe that the job appears in the local client queue.
3. Wait 30-120s; some jobs remain queued and eventually error with timeout OR disappear from client queue without printing.
4. From the print server, check active spool and filter logs for job id.

Logs/observations:
- Windows print server (windows-printsvc-03) shows frequent 'spooler timeout' entries around 10:23–10:45 on 2025-11-11.
- Printer diagnostics show occasional small packet loss on management interface (snmp ping latency spikes to 300ms) but connectivity returns.
- No DNS changes or recent network maintenance recorded in the ticket history for that time window.
- Affected users are both wired and wireless on the same VLAN; guest VLAN unaffected.

Hypothesis: Intermittent network congestion or managed switch flaps on the office floor are causing SMB/LPD sessions to drop mid-spool. Alternatively, print spooler on windows-printsvc-03 may be hitting resource limits under specific job sizes.

Temporary workaround: Ask users to re-submit smaller batched jobs (split larger docs). If urgent, use the single-office color printer with direct USB queue (manual).

Next steps (proposed):
- Engineering: collect and attach spooler logs for 2025-11-11 through 2025-11-13 (Miguel).
- Networking: enable interface error counters and port-level logging for switch-shelf-3B for 72 hours (NetOps).
- Ops: attempt a controlled restart of windows-printsvc-03 spooler during low-hours (overnight maintenance window).

Related artifacts: link to temporary diagnostics doc: drive:shared/office-it/printer-diagnostics-2025-11 (placeholder).
text:

title: Wi-Fi EAP-TLS certificate rotation fails after imaging, blocking SCCM/Intune policy application
preview: Issue summary: After new-image provisioning via SCCM/Autopilot + Intune, fleet devices intermittently fail to connect to corporate Wi-Fi. Impact: users cannot reach internal services until manual remediation; affects small percentage of newly imaged devices in US-east. Root cause suspected during certificate rotation flow: machine certificate is not present in the local machine store at time Wi‑Fi profile with EAP-TLS is applied, causing profile application to fail and network location services to stall.
text: Issue summary: After new-image provisioning via SCCM/Autopilot + Intune, fleet devices intermittently fail to connect to corporate Wi-Fi. Impact: users cannot reach internal services until manual remediation; affects small percentage of newly imaged devices in US-east. Root cause suspected during certificate rotation flow: machine certificate is not present in the local machine store at time Wi‑Fi profile with EAP-TLS is applied, causing profile application to fail and network location services to stall.

title: AuroraMed production surge: multipath fallback + autoscaler deadtime caused session resets and high-tail latency — customer restore plan & post-incident actions
preview:
Issue summary: On 2024-11-15 03:12 UTC AuroraMed began seeing elevated tail latency and session resets for multi-turn chat flows. Impact: clients experienced truncated streaming responses, duplicate completions for retrying clients, and an increased 5xx rate (service errors) for approx. 43 minutes. Multiple integrations reported out-of-order tokens and session resumption failures.

Timeline (high level):
- 03:10 UTC: first anomaly flagged by synthetic monitors (latency > 99th pct).
- 03:12 UTC: AuroraMed engineering reported customer-facing errors via support channel and opened an escalation.
- 03:15–03:32 UTC: automatic fallback routing generated multipath requests to variant models; autoscaler entered a deadtime window when scaling signals peaked and could not spin new workers fast enough.
- 03:32 UTC: mitigation step applied (temporary routing throttle and manual scale-up of dedicated pool).
- 03:55 UTC: error rate returned to baseline; 04:05 UTC: streaming sessions stabilized.

Observed impact:
- ~1.8% of AuroraMed requests got 5xx responses during window, 3x normal long-tail latency, and an elevated token duplication rate for clients with aggressive retry logic.

Why this is customer-severe: AuroraMed uses real-time clinical chat for triage; truncated responses and retries produce operator workload and potential clinical friction. This ticket captures the incident and the plan to restore customer confidence and remediate root causes.
